attributes.stem: A 25-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of intense fear and anxiety in social situations. She reports avoiding work meetings, social gatherings, and even grocery shopping due to fear of embarrassment or negative judgment from others. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. On examination, she appears anxious with a pulse rate of 100/min, blood pressure 130/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Further assessment reveals that her symptoms are significantly impacting her daily functioning and quality of life.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first-line treatment for this patient?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms consistent with social anxiety disorder, characterized by intense fear and avoidance of social situations due to fear of embarrassment or negative judgment. The most appropriate first-line treatment for this condition is cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT). CBT is a structured, time-limited therapy that helps patients identify and challenge negative thought patterns and gradually face feared situations through exposure exercises. As the patient has not had a previous trial of psychotherapy, this would be an appropriate first-line approach to avoid the burden of medication and possible side effects.

Sertraline, a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI), is considered the first-line pharmacological treatment for social anxiety disorder. While effective, it is generally recommended to start with psychotherapy, particularly CBT, before considering medication, unless the patient prefers medication or if CBT is not available or ineffective.

Graded Exposure therapy is a behavioural technique that involves gradually exposing the patient to feared social situations in a controlled manner. Graded exposure alone may not address the underlying thought patterns and beliefs that maintain social anxiety. CBT, which combines both cognitive and behavioural techniques, is generally considered more effective for social anxiety disorder.

Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) is a type of meditation therapy that has shown some promise in treating anxiety disorders, including social anxiety. While it can be helpful as an adjunct treatment, it is not considered as effective as CBT for social anxiety disorder and is not typically recommended as a first-line treatment.

Propranolol, a beta-blocker, can be useful for managing the physical symptoms of anxiety (e.g., rapid heartbeat, trembling) in specific situations like public speaking. However, it is not a first-line treatment for generalized social anxiety disorder as it does not address the underlying psychological aspects of the condition.

In summary, CBT remains the most appropriate first-line treatment for this patient with social anxiety disorder. It has the strongest evidence base for efficacy and helps patients develop long-term coping strategies without the potential side effects associated with medication. While the other options may have roles in treatment, they are not considered the optimal first-line approach for this condition.
learningPoints: Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is the first-line psychological treatment for social anxiety disorder.
SSRIs, particularly sertraline, are the first-line pharmacological treatment for social anxiety disorder.
Benzodiazepines are not recommended for long-term management of social anxiety disorder due to risk of dependence and other side effects.
references: Andrews G, Bell C, Boyce P, Gale C, Lampe L, Marwat O, Rapee R, Wilkins G. Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists clinical practice guidelines for the treatment of panic disorder, social anxiety disorder and generalised anxiety disorder. Australian & New Zealand Journal of Psychiatry. 2018 Dec;52(12):1109-72.
